item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm, I like this one. I don't have much else to say, I just felt like providing a positive voice in the discussion tab. I like articles that start with a simple object and then go in some other direction.

Easy +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yup. By the time of the later tests, both researchers have been testing this object all day and discussing how it works among themselves. They've already wondered "what happens to the original timeline when we create a paradox?" by the time the world gets ratified.

This was a bit clearer in the initial draft, which included additional researcher notes and hypotheses. It played hob with the pacing though, so I cut it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One possibility might be to call just a little more attention to the "no testing" without going overboard: "After analysis of the audio logs, further testing of SCP-4122 is forbidden."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's why they didn't figure it out the first time.

The second time, they remembered having successfully duplicated an object once, wondered why the same thing hadn't happened then — and then realized maybe it had. That noise/air-puff only happened during paradox tests, after all…

I admit that it's still quite a leap. But not unimaginable, IMO, especially from someone who's worked with parallel/split universes/timelines/what-have-you. (I doubt the currency-duplicating guy every figured it out, any of the many times he found himself being smothered by/turning into his ill-gotten gains…)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gonna no-vote this one. It's an interesting enough concept, but the final audio log doesn't make a whole lot of sense to me. It's implausible that a researcher would piece together that the horrific reality warping was caused by parallel universes/timelines/what-have-you when their own test causing said warping is a far easier conclusion to jump to. Additionally, the "this fucks up alternate universes" thing could have been inferred without it.

Otherwise it's fine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think use of redactions is more down to preference and what your personal headcanon is as to why the Foundation database is available on a creative writing site. Since other articles have the user inputting O5 credentials and reading all the Things Too Terrifying For Humanity To Know, I personally don’t see the point in redacting much at all in these articles. I use it here primarily for pacing (“redacted for brevity”).

The audio logs are transcripts of the “high pitched sound” emitted by the object every time it creates a paradox. The sound itself has to be artificially slowed down and pitched in an audible range, otherwise it just sounds like a squeak.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this SCP, but I have a few problems with it. The first one is I feel the name of the tradesman and the location of acquisition should be blacked out, as per usual with SCP article, but I suppose that's personal preference.

The other is that the audio log doesn't really seem to pertain to anything in the article. I understand that it references the 2 tests with duplicate items, but I don't see how they're really connected.

Neutral vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see what you mean. The central conceit here is that it’s a fun little curiosity with a horrifying twist ending, but until the twist hits all you’ve really got is “fun little curiosity”. It's designed to lull you into a false sense of security, but boring by design is still boring…

I can't imagine that heavier foreshadowing of the ending would help, though. O5 overreactions are a staple of poorly thought out newbie articles: I certainly don’t want to make it look like one of those.

If I can come up with a good idea for an early hook I may yet take the whole thing back to the sandbox, but for now I'll leave it up and see whether other people have the same problem with it you did.

item.title: Special Thanks
item.content:encoded:
Special thanks to TheMightyMcB for reading through an embarrassingly poorly paced first draft and helping me turn it into something worth posting, DarkStuff for helping polish off the remaining rough edges, and talkstocats for some grammatical fixes.

Additional thanks to Jacob Conwell, TheMightyMcB (again), Zyn and Doctor Staats for critiquing the initial idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've gone back and forth on this one. On one hand, the effect is relatively neatly explained (although a better mental image could definitely be painted) and the ending logs are good fun (even if I didn't understand them). In addition, the testing logs didn't feel like they were longer than they needed to be and elucidated the effect relatively well.

However, my main issue is that there's no real hook. The Containment Procedures have little substance to them, and reading the description made me think that this was just going to be yet another SCP-786, even if the concept was different. I had to force myself to continue on to the actual good part.

I'm gonna leave this with a hesitant -1 and see what the rest of the site thinks of this.
